So last time we said the premise of our series was to tell new writers not only how to make a lot of money, but how to find all the clients they'll ever need, which is really the key to making a lot of money. And we covered some of that, but Clayton, do you have additional tips to share on that topic? A few, yeah. I don't think we really got to all of them last time. Some might be a little bit repetitive, but I just want to underscore the importance of samples, whether they be spec assignments that you've completed or actual samples that you've done. I had a secretary just throw away 15 years of samples, and she's still alive, but it was an enormous loss to me. Fortunately, I'm at the point in my career where my clients have more of my samples than I did anyway. So I'm in an okay shape there, but samples are solid gold to a writer. Sending them out to anybody and everybody that you could possibly want to work for with a hard-hitting cover letter is how I got started. And even though my samples were in another area, I was wanting to write for newsletters and all my samples were about fundraising or water heaters or a bakery in Beverly Hills. They could see the strength of the copy, and I got assignments as a result. That's a real big key. So that's an important point you're saying. Obviously, it's better if the samples can be in the same area, but if they're not, you can get an assignment from a client in product area A, even if your samples are in products B, C, and D, and you have no experience in A, if they're good samples. Absolutely. If the copy is strong, they'll be able to spot it right off the bat. Quite frankly, outside of the information publishing industry, if you're wanting to write for other kinds of companies, they're used to seeing samples from advertising companies and copy writers that have nothing to do with their specific niche. And so it seems quite natural for them to be reviewing that kind of material. And in our industry, in newsletters books and so forth, in the financial and health areas, our clients are savvy enough to be able to spot great copy when they see it. And they understand that younger writers and beginning writers haven't had the opportunity to write in their industry before, and they're highly motivated to find the next hot copy writers. So their minds will be open to this kind of thing. Having said that, once you have a sample, let's say you do have a sample in the client's industry or product. Is that better to send? What's the best sample to send? Obviously, it would be the samples that are closest to his area of interest. But because that way you begin developing your niche. I've required that all of my clients send me a FedEx with 25 samples of every package that I do. Is that in your verbal or written agreement with them? It's just verbal. But the other thing is, I have one of these great new handy dandy, $299 does everything but wash your socks, fax machines, copiers, scanners sitting on my desktop. Only cost a couple hundred dollars. And I can scan and make PDFs of my packages. In some cases, clients do. And that gives you an inexhaustible supply of samples that you can send out instantly to anyone in the country. So that's really important. I think another key is the networking key. We touched on it last time in the total package also. We talked about how Paris Lampropolis took samples that were completely unrelated to the newsletter industry along with a hot cover letter and walked into a conference where there were clients who were looking for copywriters. Walked away with several assignments, a thousand, ten to thousands of dollars in advances, and a new client at the time for him who's now become his main client, SoundView publications down in Atlanta. And so these conferences have much greater value than a lot of people really attribute to them. Another key to attending these kinds of conferences, and this is something I'd encourage every younger copywriter or beginning copywriter to do. And they give you an opportunity to meet other copywriters. And the magic that happens at the conferences during, like for example at the AWAI boot camp, the peer review sessions, can happen for you every day on the phone in terms of your copywriting. Find a partner. Find somebody who'll crit your copy for you and whose copy you can crit. And together you can learn and grow. That's how Gary Benaventga got started. He was with David Ogilvy's agency, left there, paired up with Dan Rosenthal, who was also a young writer at the time. And the two of them critted each other's copy and sharpened each other. And the result was that both of them became multi-million dollar copywriters. So those kinds of pairings are really important. And also at those kinds of events, don't be afraid to approach and talk to people who are more senior than you, senior copywriters. They absolutely will talk to you. When I was starting and I was 22 or 23, I was a kid. I had no experience. I was a chemical engineer. I went to one of these events. My first one, and Ed McLean, most of the people on the call who know who he is, recently passed away, was unbelievably gracious to me and spent so much time with me for nothing in return. It was nothing I could give him. Well, it's an incredible opportunity. Right now, I haven't even counted them all. I probably have 15 open assignments on my desk from seven or eight different major mailers. I can't possibly get all of that work. And just in the last 30 days, I've hired four young copycubs whose samples I like. Their samples, most of them weren't even in the particular areas that I needed copy on. I think I mentioned this to you the other day, Bob. The education that you get from AWAI and from other sources on how to write copy is absolutely invaluable. It's like going to Harvard and getting your bachelor's, your master's, and finally your MD and graduating medical school. But after you leave there, you don't just hang up a shingle and start operating on people. You go into an internship where you work under the auspices of a practicing physician. And in the copywriting field, that can be an enormous advantage for you as well to take your training and use it to get a position with a senior writer who needs help, who has more work than he can do, and who can coach you on real-world projects and serve an internship of a few months to a year under that writer. When you're done then, what do you have? You have a year's worth of samples that you can show people and say Clayton Makepeace or Carleen Angleick Cole or Paris Lampropoulos and I wrote this together. And that is like just busting the door down at these major mailers. Now the immediate question that's raising in everyone's mind is, that sounds great. I would like to intern for senior copywriter. But not all senior copywriters do this. Some do and some don't. How do I make that happen? Is that through the networking? How do I make that connection? Well, I think networking is making the connection. Beyond that, more and more senior writers are beginning to do this. Arthur Johnson, I hear is going to be doing this over at Filz Financial. I'm doing it with the Healthy Directions and several other clients. Paris Lampropoulos is doing it at SoundView. And his word gets out. Steve Wexler does it and he's on the web. He does it for nutritional supplements. And as more and more writers do it and demonstrate how profitable it can be for the senior and how beneficial it is for the copy cup, it's something that's really gaining momentum right now. And the key is, again, that you come away with a solid real-world experience, writing copy, dealing with clients, getting the research, and winding up with samples that you can really use to fill your dance card in the months ahead. So I mean, Donald Trump has publicized this concept widely used that you use the metaphor of an intern or a resident in medicine, but it's really being an apprentice, right? That's exactly what it is. And you have much less of a chance of having Trump call up and tell you you're fired. You use one term just so everyone's on the same page that people may not know a piece of jargon. You said, crit. Can you explain? That's short for criticism, right? That's just short for critique. Some people think it's short for criticism. That's too negative for me. Critique is both the negative and positive aspects of the copy, showing you where it's weak, but also helping you strengthen those areas and providing additional ideas for headlines and texts and sale points and so forth that can really take response to the moon on packages you're working on. Also the aspect of dealing with clients is critical here because that's a whole art in itself and something that you can only learn by doing. That's terrific. Any other advice on how you can get more clients? You talked about networking, you talked about having samples. You haven't talked about the Internet. What do you think? I always get asked this, should I have a website? A lot of top copywriters don't have websites. Should new copywriters put up websites? There are a lot of things that top copywriters don't do. The first thing that I never did was to ask for testimonials from clients. With my history of dropping out a site focusing on one client, not promoting myself across the marketplace or even letting people know who I was, you put up a blog on your blog site where you said who are the top copywriters in America. I couldn't help but notice. I listed my suggestion and you were one of them. Yes, you did. I appreciate that. In reading the responses from your people, it was shocking to me to discover that so many of them had no clue as to who I was. The people they named were more of the people who are active promoters. That's right. Not all of the active promoters are top copywriters. Getting your name out there is very important. Having a website is, I think, in this day and age crucial. However, a major caveat here, in everything that you do when you're searching for clients, it's absolutely essential to remember that every word that you write to a client about introducing yourself or your past successes or why they should hire you is sales copy. People don't realize they're self-promotions or samples. That's right. I go to some of these websites and many of them are stiff. Many of them are stilted. Written more like a technical writer would write something rather than a sales copywriter. So yes, by all means, do a website. You can throw them up for a couple of hundred bucks these days. But make sure that the copy on the website itself is representative of the power that you can bring to your sales copy. Another thing to do is that I've never done this. Six months ago, I was planning to launch the total package. So I sent out an email to clients that I had worked for over the years. Some of them I hadn't talked to in a decade or longer. I just asked them if they would be so kind as to write a couple of paragraphs about what I had done for them or the value that I brought to their companies over the years. I was absolutely shocked. Everybody was so nice. I didn't think I'd get that many comments. I was interrupting their busy days. Some of these people are running a hundred million, two hundred million dollar companies. And yet, Tom Phillips, Brian Kurtz, Martin Weiss, all of these people, Kim Giffler at the Money Show, all sent me these wonderful long five, six, seven paragraphs long test testimonials. Gary Bencevenga wrote, my competitors wrote great testimonials. You did. Bencevenga did. Rutz did. And so those testimonials then bring instant credibility to anything that I say about myself, whether on a website or in an email or in a FedEx package with samples that I send to clients. So as soon as you have some successes, and I do it now with every single client I have, when I have a successful package, I ask for a testimonial. I do that too. And I post them on my website. And if people go to my website, which is my last name, www.blighbsnboyly.com, click on testimonials and you'll see page after page after page. And it's not only the quality of them. The actual quantity is important. If it's more than people would want to read, that's good because they just get overwhelmed. They say, all right, enough already. That's right. And they say, if this many people say this, this guy must be good. So that's great. So we're talking about how do we get lots of clients? We've given some great techniques. Networking, testimonials, which everyone should get, putting up a website, you know, copy cubbing or apprenticing or interning with a more experienced copywriter. You also talked about this strategy the last time. You say, look for local companies that should be using direct marketing but aren't. Because most of your clients that you work for are major direct marketers. But if you're a beginner, I think you can get some really great samples and do great work for companies that aren't direct marketers but might want to do a sales letter. And I've done a lot of that. Yeah, absolutely. You did something like that. You said with boilers or water heaters. Yeah. And the compensation doesn't always have to be financial on these things. I mean, of course you have to pay the bills. But sometimes there are companies who don't want to lay out cash but will trade in kind services in return for copy. At one point in the 70s, early 70s, I decided I want to learn how to fly. And I was making a good enough income that I could just barely pay the bills. But I certainly didn't have, you know, five or $10,000 to pay for flight instruction and then to rent airplanes and fly. And so I just sent a letter out to the flight schools in Los Angeles and told them, you know, the letter just said, look, you want new client, new flight students and new charters for your aircraft. I want to learn how to fly. You don't want to pay for marketing. I don't want to pay to learn how to fly. There's a deal to be made here. And I sent that letter out on a Friday. Saturday morning I got a phone call. And one of the best flight schools in LA had said, had written back or had called me and said, you know, don't talk to anyone else. Come over here right now and talk to us. We need you. And I walked out of there with a $20,000 credit on their books and took my first flight less in that day. Another writer, a good friend of mine, Daniel Rosenthal, is famous for going into a company that isn't familiar with direct marketing or direct response and how much money it can make them. And simply saying, look, I've got an offer you can't refuse. I want to do a promotion for you. And all I want you to do is give me 30% of sales. 30% of sales. I know one promotion he did that made him $4 million using that kind of a deal. He simply went to a company and said, let me write a sales letter for you. I think if I remember right, it was only eight pages long. It took him a week or two to get it perfect. They mailed it out and his 30% got him $4 million. So finding companies that aren't using direct marketing or don't fully understand the value of strong copy and then talking to them about hiring you on spec for a percentage of sales is great because it's always easy for them to sign an agreement saying that they're going to give you 30% of this huge new number that just boggles their mind. It's the easiest sale in the world to make. You're saying to them, look, I'm going to give you $9 million in Rosenthal's case. He was saying, I'm going to give you what's the math on that? Twelve, I guess. Twelve million dollars. Roughly. Yeah. Of that, you've got to pay me four. All the client heard was, you're going to give me $8 million in the next 30 days. And he did the deal. So that's the old J.A. Abraham when he teaches this principle of selling. He says, you go it up someone and say, will you give me a quarter if I'll give you a quarter? Now, you mentioned going to companies that don't use direct marketing and then creating a huge success for them that they wouldn't have. But what about the idea of, I've heard this suggested by amateurs, they say, why don't I go look for direct mail packages from direct marketers, companies, newsletter publishers or whatever, seminar promoters, book publishers, that I see a lot of them that aren't so good. Should I approach them about saying, hey, your piece isn't that good. I can beat it and then get a job that way. Does that work? That's what I call being a direct response bully. We covered that in pretty good detail on the call. Pay special attention to that section. It really is a great way. It helped me in my early career and it's an offer that clients quite often just can't refuse. Now, this all sounds well and good, but I'm introverted myself and I'm shy and a lot of people have told me at copywriting boot camps, seminars, well, I'd be very nervous or shy or intimidated about contacting a potential client if I started as a freelancer. I don't know if you consider yourself an introvert or an extrovert, but regardless, how do you do that? How do you get over that? Well, it just helps to understand that the people that hire writers are essentially charged with the task of hiring writers, of finding great new writers and knowing that these people are just dying to find the next hot copywriter for their company. The one missing ingredient that they have is good sales copy. If they had more writers, they could introduce more products, they could grow the products that they have now more quickly. It's the one thing they can't get. It's the one scarce resource for them. They can hire editorial people to create editorial products until sundown. They can find people to work in their company, no problem. Finding a copywriter who could sell their product is the one thing they're missing. I think just having the confidence of knowing that these people's personal success in life is tied to finding you gives you a level of confidence in talking to these people that should push you over the top. You've said in the previous call that the supply and demand balance of copywriting to people who need copy is heavily weighted in your favor of the supply, the copywriter. That's certainly true for you, but is this true for people who are less experienced or some of the starting or novice copywriters on this call? Well, it's true for everybody. The only question is, who's going to hire you? There's no doubt that the supply and demand equation is tilted very heavily in the favor of the writer. This is a writer's market. Like in real estate, they say this is a seller's market. In the direct response industry, the entire industry is a copywriter's market. As I said before, they need us. By the way, would agree with this for people listening. I don't have ... Clayton mentioned that he had seven or eight jobs that he's got to assign out. I mean, I turn away and a lot of people at AEWI have asked to be on my referral list because I refer leads to other people, but I turn away and I've always told people this for years, we turn away four to five assignments for everyone we take. Absolutely. On the referral side, it's hard to know who to refer to because, for example, I called Kim Krause the other day. I wanted to get on her dance card for early next year. She doesn't have an open slot till July. The thing here is that the very fact that I'm hiring writers who are AWI graduates and in most cases have nothing more than spec assignments to show means that there are opportunities out there. Also, as I said earlier, our clients are just looking for some indication that you can do a good job on the copy that you're writing. It's hard to believe. I know a lot of our listeners may not believe this, but the fact of the matter is that although there are hundreds more beginning writers today than ever before thanks to AEWI and some of the things that Bob and I have done and others, Gary Halbert and John Carlton and Gary Benaventga are doing, that's attracted more young writers to the field. But of those young writers, I think you're going to find that they fall into several groups. Some have a dream, but will never quite take the bit in their mouth and go out there and find clients. Others are easily discouraged and the first time or two that they go out to try to find clients and they're unable to get an assignment, they'll just give up. That's a tragedy because you've got to work through those times. You've got to learn how to deal with the rejection that comes along from time to time with being a copywriter. It's never nasty, it's never mean-spirited, but you spend all this time getting ready, you send out your sample and if somebody isn't enthusiastic about hiring you right off the bat, some people just get discouraged and that's terrible, but it's true. The people who will succeed are the ones who have a reasonable expectation going forward, understanding that they have the skills, the talents, the training to get started, they need to spend some time doing practical work in the real world and internship or residency if you want to call it that and pick up skills that way, make contacts, network, do all of these things. The money will come. I'm living proof of it and every other copywriter that I've introduced you to in the Total Package and that you've heard of and Bob himself will attest to the fact that as I said at the AI, it ain't all of that erosive, you have to be willing to persevere, but if you do that, the money's there, it will come. Well it's like in the movie A League of the Roan where Gina, whatever her name is, Davis I think complains to Tom Hanks, she says, well I don't want to do this, it's really hard, he says of course it's hard, that's the good part, if it was easy everyone would do it. There's no crying in baseball. Yeah, there's no crying in baseball. Now we've got a couple of emails coming in that I have some good questions. I've signed up for your tele-seminars, I'm listening now, and in these calls aren't you guys, isn't Clayton mostly talking about us, just a small handful of clients, Agora, Phillips, Boardroom, who pay royalties based on head-to-head competition between top copywriters to be to control or is most of this information just as applicable to the majority of copywriting clients. So what say you? I think we've touched on that. The demand for copywriters is absolutely massive out there. Everybody from your local dry cleaner to your local car dealership to your local shops need advertising and whether it's print ads, direct mail or internet promotions, the one thing that's restricting their growth is lack of strong sales copy. And so just like I started out writing for local fundraising organizations, local bakeries, local manufacturers, local restaurants, there are opportunities in every one of those areas. I live in the tiniest little town imaginable. We have a company here that sells paint, we have a company here that sells technology, and we have a company here that sells packaging, boxes and shipping cartons. Every one of those companies needs more clients. They have the capacity to produce the product. What they don't have is enough people calling them up and doing deals with them to buy their product. That's B2B, but people at work are motivated by the same things that people at home are. They want to be successful. So you're saying these guys could be approached and instead of a flat B, you can offer them a Dan Rosenfielder type deal. So you'll say, I'll write this for you in exchange for some percentage of the increase in sales. Absolutely. If you allow yourself to get locked into the flat C thing with these local companies, here's what happens. They right now have the local cable guy, the local newspaper guy, the local TV and radio guys banging on their doors and saying you need to advertise. And if you buy time or you buy space from those sales people, they will write your ad for you free. And that ad is exactly worth what you paid for. It's worth nothing. And those ads don't work for these people and they try them for a while and then they say, oh, advertising doesn't work. Marketing doesn't work. The problem wasn't the medium. The problem was the message. And so when you go into those places and you offer them to come in and write something and give them a fee, $2,000 to write an ad or $5,000 or $10,000 to write a direct mail piece, their toes are going to curl up. Copy isn't worth anything. I'm getting it free right now. So give it to them free. Give it to them free. Just say, look, OK, here's what we'll do. I'll write this package for you. And I want 10, 20, 30, pick your number, percent of the net sales from that ad. Right? Well, to them, you just said, I'm going to write free and you're not going to have to pay me anything ever because advertising doesn't work. So how can they turn that down? You're offering to give them a week or a month or two months of your time, whatever it takes to get this done. And they don't have to pay you upfront and they're probably not going to have to pay you anything at all because advertising doesn't work as far as they're concerned. So that's tremendous opportunity. I did something like this with Guthrie Ranker. They had hundreds of thousands of people who had bought their products, including Vanna White's Tooth Whitening Kit. Once they sold the main product, they never contacted those people again because they had no frame of reference on how direct mail works. And so we structured a whole department in the company that did nothing but direct mail promotions to people who had bought Vanna's products. And each sales letter was signed by Vanna and they got a monthly newsletter and sales soared. So, again, the key is think about the frame of reference of your potential client and then think about the easiest way to sell to him that will break you out of that, that fee, you know, that strict fee structure. As a copywriter, you're a salesperson. The most important product you'll ever sell is yourself. And so you need to spend time every month thinking about the offers that you can make. When you write for a product, the offer is a key component of your promotion. When you're writing for yourself, the same is true. You need to think about the offer and structure offers that potential clients find it difficult if not impossible to say no to. Now we're getting emails that are of two camps. The first camp, and Julie, I want your feedback on this, the first camp is that, gee, I'm sending my emails and I've sent several and you guys aren't getting to them. And the second camp is you guys are starting to answer emails and you haven't gotten through all the agenda yet. Actually, I wanted to interrupt quickly too because I am seeing a common question here and I've seen it over and over again for a while now, actually. You are really concerned about getting ripped off. They want to make sure that they know how they can hold their clients accountable for what they're owed. Can you talk about that a little bit? Yeah, I get that question a lot. Before I answer it, I want to say that we will cover all of the agenda items today on this call. Should we, for any reason, fall short because we're answering current questions through emails or whatever, we will hold additional agenda items over until the next call and we will keep doing this until the questions are answered. We will get to every agenda item on this call. If any are left over, you will get them on the next call and you will get every agenda item we promised for all four calls and we will answer every question that we get. We may add a little more time at the end to do that and we won't charge you extra, but you'll get everything. Absolutely. I'm actually seeing also that even though a specific question with a person who's answering it is not necessarily being asked, they're being answered. Even if it doesn't seem like a question is being answered, a lot of the questions that are coming through are actually being answered in your conversation. Great. Now, let's go back to that. How do you prevent getting ripped off? You make a deal with the local paint guy that you described and he said, you say, 10% of sales and he mails your letter and you never hear from him. The key there, of course, is trust. First off, I have always required the clients that I do this deal with and it's not an unreasonable request to make simply because you're putting your time on the line and it's costing the client very little or nothing. Very reasonable request to make is to establish a benchmark for what they're advertising is doing for them now and then to include you on the daily reports or weekly reports, sales reports showing what your promotion is doing. Those reports should come directly from their order department and that will give you some sense of assurance. I can assure people that it is not worth the time and trouble for them to cook a second set of books just for a copywriter. They're not going to do it. No, it's not and it exposes them to legal liability as well. It's not going to happen. There's another factor there too you have to be aware of. Please remember what I said earlier, you are the essential missing ingredient. If you write a promotion for this company and it blows the doors off everything they've ever done and it brings in millions of dollars or thousands of dollars they otherwise wouldn't have earned, do you think they're going to do anything at all to risk alienating you? At that point, it's not a one-time job anymore. These people are going to do everything in their power to get you to sign a long-term contract to make sure that you do this work for them every month and continue producing these results. I had that very specific experience at Security Rare Coin where I wrote one monthly promotion on the if-come to his house file people. Got him ten times the response he normally would get and as soon as the money started coming in, this guy was on the phone to me offering to pay moving expenses, quarter of a million dollar a year salary, a new house in Minneapolis and five percent of gross sales if I would just come there and build a direct marketing department for him. A person would be a fool to do anything to make you question whether or not they're paying you a fair royalty. We included sample written agreements in our course materials. If not, because someone's asked about, we're talking about the email I just got is talking about how to contact people is great, but how do we secure the deal? What should a contract look like? What should be included? I've got actually on my website. If you go to Bly.com, don't do it now, but do it after the call and you click on the main homepage, www.Bly.com. You click on a button called methodology which explains how I work. One of those menu choices that will pop up at the bottom is my standard agreement. I can't give it to you as a legal contract because I'm not a lawyer, but you can see what I use. I think it's a pretty good standard agreement. That's great. In preparation for this also, I decided something I really haven't mentioned to the Redhead or Julie or you. That is that I have years worth of contracts in my files that I've signed with many clients. They cover many different kinds of deals. They cover flat fee plus royalty, advance plus royalty, overrides on ongoing sales from new customers I generate. What I want to do is I want to take those contracts and black out the names of the customers or my clients and also the specific percentages and just give you rough guidelines on those. That way you can see the actual deal points and the actual copy that lawyers were paid, the contracts that lawyers were paid thousands of dollars to create. You can pattern contracts that you write on that. There will be two kinds of situations, three actually. There are clients that basically do business on a handshake deal. If it's an established company with a good reputation for paying royalties or commissions or just paying your bills to copywriters, then you can usually feel pretty comfortable with that. The other kind of deal is the deal where the customer, the client has a standing contract. That will be in situations where it's a company that's used to hiring writers often. In that situation, they have standard contracts that they send out. That can be a little daunting because this is something that appears to be cast in stone. This is how much we pay. This is a royalty. This is when you get paid your royalty. These are the clauses for if you fail to perform or blah, blah, blah. Every one of those points is negotiable. Don't allow yourself to just look at it, sign it and send it back. Go through the points, think about each one. Pay attention to the minutia on those. I'm going to take those contracts and we're going to create a PDF. We will get you a PDF of real contracts and you can see mine immediately online. That will answer that question. We understand that we can ask for royalties, but why would a client pay or how could a beginning copywriter ask for the same royalties that a Paris or Dan or Rosenthal or Clayton get? Why would a client pay me as a beginner top dollar? Because the customer that you generate for him has the same value to the client as a customer that I generate or Paris generates. The royalty is the one part of the fee arrangement that should be absolutely equal no matter who's doing the work. After these days, clients who are really trying to get the best writers they possibly can't will standardize their deals. Instead of paying me 50 bucks a thousand and paying someone else 30 and then getting away with paying somebody else 15, there will be a standing royalty rate that everybody will be offered. The thing is that your royalty, even at $50 a thousand, even if you were able to get 50, which I usually do for my financial clients, it's only 10% of his cost of direct mail. Even if your royalty is 10% of the net on internet sales, it's only 10% of the net. It's the most important component in his company's growth and yet it only costs him 10%. The royalty itself is chicken feed. The fact is that the money you produce for him and the clients you produce for him, the new customers you produce for him, have every bit as much value as the money and the customers that I generate or anyone else does. Guys, I want to read something that just came through because it's just really incredible and it really brings home what you're saying. This is from Mark in Canada, actually. He says, I came in touch with the AWAI Copywriting Course around 15 months ago and studied your business model. In May of this year, I took the plunge and quit my job to become a full-time independent copywriter. The result? In June, I only earned $3,000. I broke even in July and August and in September, I earned $12,000. This month, I will make nearly $20,000 and I have worked up into January and I haven't even completed my copywriting website yet. I think I know who this is, so to my friend Mark Kay, we won't reveal your last name, congratulations. Really, I'm proud of you. That's wonderful but not a surprise. This is somebody who learned his craft and who then followed his dream and stuck with it and month one, $3,000, okay, fine. But now he's almost seven times that amount and he's booked up. That's the reality of the situation. Go ahead, Clint. I want to make one very brief other point about the royalty situation. A royalty is a no risk deal to the client. If you don't kick the living daylights out of his control, I don't care if your deal says you need $1,000 or 1,000 royalty. It's not going to cost him a dime if you don't beat his control. Getting yourself to be negotiated down on the royalty rates is just specious. It's ridiculous. There's no reason to do it. It's easy to be intimidated into it and if you are and I have been from time to time, that's fine but it's you spending your own money at that point. What about you mentioned standard practices? I called one very well-known direct marketer, or they called me rather a couple of years ago and I asked about this and there was a statement very terse from the marketing director who's a wonderful person. We don't pay royalties and that was the end of the discussion. I know who that is and that was a blatant lie because they'd been paying me royalties for about seven years at that point. Cursus Alley. The fact of the matter is there are a couple of companies that say that but the fact of the matter is when it gets right down to it, if they want to get on a writer's dance card, who charges royalties, the only way to do that is to pay the royalty. A friend of mine sent out an email earlier, probably about a month ago. Now this friend has been in the copywriting area now for several years and makes six figures and it's my suggestion because the problem was they were getting way too many requests for work than they could possibly handle and potential clients were getting frustrated because they weren't able to get on the writer's dance card. So I said look, you can hand it, it was becoming a distraction to the writer and so I just said look, it's very simple, all you do is you send out an email, you double your fees, you're asking $15,000 right now in advance, ask $30,000 and instead of $25,000 a thousand royalties, ask $50,000. Then two things are going to happen. First of all, the volume of this pestering is going to stop. And secondly, if you do get a client who calls and says okay, fine, now you've doubled your income. So this writer who trusts me implicitly, which may or may not be a mistake, I don't know, said exactly what I said that hour. Within an hour I got a call from one of the major mailers out there who said do you know what so-and-so is doing? And I said no, what? And they told me, oh, this is alright? I said well, you know, they're booked up. And what it comes right down to is if you want their work, you're going to have to pay their fee. And long story short, this writer has since, who was already booked up well into next year, has accepted two assignments at double their normal rate. So it's one of those situations, write great copy, learn your craft, write great copy, and then let your clients raise your fees for you. So obviously one way I can make each client more profitable for me is just to double or just raise my rates, but you've got some other ways. How else can you make more money from the same clients? One of the things I learned early on, Bob King and I used to talk about this, is that the time I'm most ready to write a package for a product, to write a promotion for a given product, is five minutes after I've just delivered a package for that product. I usually will have tons of ideas I wasn't able to work into the text, and tons of copies that I've had to clip out just so the thing would be the proper length. And plus I've had my mind totally devoted to that product for weeks, and I know that product inside out, I know the market inside out, writing about it is effortless at that point, the learning curve has already been played out. So the best time for me to write a second package for a product is right after I wrote the first one. And so I quite often will tell a client, you know, and you know, Peter or Eric Battelle does this too. I'll often tell a client, look, yes, I want to write for this product. I need to tell you a couple of things. Quite often the first package I write for a new product does well, but it's not a home run because it's my first package for the product. Quite often the second package I write for a product blows the doors off, anything that's done before, for all the reasons I just cited. And so instead of selling one at a time, I say, I want to do two packages in rapid sequence for this product. The first one I'll deliver on such and such a date. And then two weeks later, I'll deliver you first draft copy for a second one. I'll charge you a $25,000 advance for the first one and a $12,500 advance for the second one. Fifty percent of the first. Fifty percent of the first. Which is what Don Mahoney, co-founder of AWAI does. He uses the same technique. It doesn't always work, but it works often enough that it's worth doing. It's worth trying. And the fact that that second package is out there gives you more than doubles your chances of getting a control and getting royalties. Doubles your chances of getting a control and royalties and you've increased the upfront sale 50 percent. That's right. Well in my case, the upfront sale is up 75 because I got full rate on the first package and half rate on the second. Another way to do it is, and this is difficult to do when you're at the very beginning, but once your schedule starts filling up and as we just heard from your friend at Canada, it can happen fairly quickly in a matter of months. You can simply say to the client, I'm booking my schedule for the next six months and I only have four slots left. How many do you want? More often than not, the client will come back and say, I want two or I want three or I want four and all of a sudden you're getting $30,000, $40,000, $50,000 in advance this month for package for work you won't have to do for several months. And that's a great script and people should use it exactly as you said. I'm booking my schedule for the next six months. I have four slots left. How many do you want? Not do you want one, but how many do you want? You never want to ask a yes or no question. I did that once. Wendy will remember when. I was in the 90s, the early 90s if I remember right. With Bordrum, I had just written my first package for them. It was the first Magalock they had ever done. And it was for a product called The Big Black Book, which was an old product that had been around forever. And the response in the mail to that product was steadily declining. They were about to retire the product permanently and they said, well, what the heck? We got this writer we've never used before, Clayton Minkpees. He wants to do something we've never done before, which is a Magalock. What do we have to lose? Let's have him try this product out. I wrote the copy. We mailed it. The response came in at as high as it was when the book was brand new. It completely revitalized the product. Instead of having to invent a new product to grow their sales, they had an old product now that was suddenly new again. They were absolutely thrilled. They just could hardly sit still. I sent them an email saying, great, I'm really happy that the package worked well. I'm scheduling up the rest of my year. I need to tell you that I've already filled four slots and I'm getting calls from customers every single day wanting more copy. But based on this success and the amount of money I'm going to make on this with you, I would really prefer to give Boardroom first shot at these packages. If I hear from you in the next 24 hours, we'll set that up. If I don't, then I'm going to go ahead and send out my customer email to the rest of my clients offering them these slots for the rest of the year. The next day, Boardroom called, took every slot I had available for the year, which was eight slots, and cut me a check for almost $100,000 in advances, which is 50% of the $25,000 that I charge. I collect, and this is important, when you're invoicing for advances, let's say your advance is $25,000. Typically what the client will want to do is pay you half of that advance when you begin the work and half of that advance when you deliver acceptable final copy. I don't like doing it that way because too many times over the years, I've had customers say, yeah, I want you in April. In April comes along, and there's no research kit, or they've canceled the product, or the premium that's supposed to drive the package has proven to be a dud, and so they have no work for you, and you're sitting there with nothing to do in the month of April. So what I require is that my clients pay half of my $25,000 advance the minute they schedule the work. That's what we do. We say the slot we've promised is not held until the deposit is received. Absolutely right. Then that 50% of your total advance is also, and I'll show you this in the contracts you'll receive, is also a kill fee. It's nonrefundable. It's nonrefundable. If you and the client can't work together, you can't get along. If the chemistry is wrong. If they cancel the project and never have it. If they cancel the project, if they're not ready with research on time, if they don't respond with crits to the copy in a timely manner, you simply resign the assignment, keep the $12.5, and walk away. That almost never happens because if that causes in the contract, the client's going to make darn sure that he's got everything you need to get you going on time. So that's very important. On the other end of the cycle too, when you're dealing with clients on one season, two season, collecting the second half of the advance when the copy is final is fine. But if you've got a long-term relationship with a client, they know that you write killer copy and you've had some successes with them, I demand payment of the second half of the advance when I submit first draft copy. The client knows that I'm motivated to get the copy final because I don't collect royalties until it's final. And so I have plenty of incentive to not let the thing ride after I deliver first draft. But there's no reason at that point really not to collect the money for the second half of the advance as soon as your first draft copy is submitted. So in the contract, you have to be very specific about the payment terms. When you get paid, the invoices, etc., what else should I look for or worry about in my copywriting contract that you're going to send me samples of? All right. Well, in the ads that we ran for this call series, we talk about landmines to avoid in a copywriter contract. And there are five major ones and then a few minor ones. The first one is ownership of the copy. Marketing companies have sent me contracts that say they own the copy. They can do any darn thing they want with it as long as they want with it. And my compensation is tied just to, for example, direct mail use of the copy. Ownership of the copy is fine. Allowing the client to own the copy is fine. So long as there's also a clause in the contract that says that every use of that copy will result in a payment to you. And then working through with the client how the copy might be used. You're writing a direct mail package. Are they going to email it? Are they going to turn it into a print ad? Are they going to exert to your copy for a television or radio script? Well, if so, then there needs to be clauses in the contract that make sure that you get paid for every use of that copy. And the payment, again, for non-direct mail use where you're going for quantity and your royalty is tied to the number of pieces mailed, the compensation agreement for television and radio and the internet are quite different because they're cheaper media and there's a greater likelihood that the client will make money using your copy in those media. And so in those situations, instead of going on a royalty per thousand pieces mailed or per thousand impressions, I prefer to go with a commission on net sales. So what would that commission be? Let's say for email or web copy. My standard is 10 percent. Of net? Mm-hmm. Okay. And net on many internet promotions, for example, is really very close to the growth. Yeah, because there's no marketing costs to speak of and they tend to deliver their products online so they don't have to print and mail products. So that's very close. When I'm selling products such as supplements or anything on television or radio, there is a hard cost to fulfillment to shipping the product and having the product made and then shipping it. And the costs of promotion are higher. And so, of course, you have to examine what your net would be per sale. And in cases where there's a big question as to whether the more profitable way would be to go with a royalty on impressions or pieces mailed or a percentage of sales, I simply have a clause in the contract that says compensation will be based on the greater of the two formulas that follow. And the first one will be a royalty formula and the second one will be a commission on sales formula. So we've got the ownership of the copy, the compensation terms. We've talked about the payment terms. What about penalty clauses? Well, I want to get back to compensation terms because there are a couple of other things there. One of the tricks that some mailers will use, for example, is that they will bury a clause in the contract that says that you get no royalty on test lists. I think that's totally unfair. If they're mailing to a test list, they have an equal chance of making a bundle on that list or losing money. In other words, the odds are they're going to break even most of the time on those test lists. And so you deserve the same royalty on when they test a new file with your package as you do when they're rolling out to other files. Believe it or not, there's some major mailers out there who will still insist on that. And it's a negotiating point. I would stick by my guns as much as possible on that because there are so many ways for that to be abused. They could do massive test size on lists that they think are going to work, for example, and not pay you for it. So it's better just to have a very clear, simple thing. Look, anytime you mail it, I get the royalty, period. So let me interrupt for a second, Julie. We have a large, ambitious agenda, which fortunately or unfortunately, we're only about 60% of the way through. And it's 1 o'clock. And plus we have a ton of email questions that are piling up, some of which we've answered and some of which we haven't. I'm thinking, if it's OK now, I'm thinking, Julie, we should continue today for another 15 minutes if people are willing to stick with us. And if not, they can still hear it right on MP3. It's not like they'll miss out. And we'll tell them how to do that, or you'll tell them how to do that. And so my plan, if everyone agrees, is we'll go another 15 minutes and we'll continue with the pace we're going because this is great information. And 115 will wrap. Whatever we didn't cover on this agenda, that will be the beginning of our next call. And whatever questions we didn't answer, we'll also get answered during the next call and subsequent call. So we will get through all the agenda and we will answer all the questions. Does that work for you guys? Yeah. And I just want to tell everyone that's listening, please be patient with us. We're trying to give you as much information as possible and as practical information as possible. And you have my word that by the time the series is over, you will have had the answers to everything we promised you and a lot, lot more. Don't get panic-stricken. If you have something that you want to know and you feel uncomfortable because we haven't gotten to it yet or it hasn't been answered, email it to us and we are keeping these all in a file and everything will be answered. Okay. Getting back to the contract again, let me go through the other points quickly. Contractor terms are very important, not just how much but when you get paid. There are companies, a lot of companies will say, well, we pay once a quarter. And a company just pitched me that kind of a deal the other day. And I wrote back and with a twinkle in my eye and just said, hey, look guys, when I loan people money, I expect interest. If you're going to sit on my royalty payments for three months at a time and age them, I mean, let's not kid each other. We know what you're doing. You're aging these royalty payments. If you're going to age my royalty payments, I want interest. Otherwise, I want you to pay me monthly when, based on your mailing receipts or even on your merge purge. A merge purge is when a company rents many mailing lists. There will be duplicate names on those lists. The company sends a tape or a disc of all of the names that they've rented to a computer house or does it internally. And they run those lists against each other and identify the duplicate names. Those names are then removed from the most expensive lists first to lower their cost of mailing and then from all of the other lists. So they end up with a clean list with no duplicates. They know exactly what their mail quantity is. And that's what you're paid on? And there's no reason to age your royalties one day past the day that they know what the mail quantity will be. It may be reasonable in some cases for them to say, yeah, but wait. We'll pay you the day we mail and we'll provide you with a postal receipt showing you how many we mail. That's fine too. But this business of sitting on your royalty for three months is just their way of nickel and diming you. It makes their books look good. It makes their accountants look like they're looking out for the company. It costs you money and disincentivizes you to work for that company. So that's something to look for. Penalty clauses and contracts. Some contracts you'll come across and they'll say, well, if you're one day late, we're going to charge you $50,000. And they'll say, well, if you're one day late, we're going to charge you 10% of the back-end payment or whatever. There'll be some performance guarantee and tie it to a timeline or a deadline. I avoid those at all terms. In all cases, I will not accept that in a contract because that's like walking up to a painter, a great painter, and saying you have to paint a masterpiece in this many days and if it takes you one day more, you're going to get paid because you want to make it perfect. You're going to get paid less. Jim Rutt once said that the heartbreak of a blown deadline is quickly forgotten in the warm glow of a hot new control. The customer wants the hottest copy possible. You want to give them the hottest copy possible. Artificial deadlines are an obstacle to doing that. And any penalty, any time they put in a clause that penalizes you for trying to give them the hottest copy possible is counterproductive for both of you. So absolutely avoid those. We already talked about kill fees. That's an essential contract point. Not every deal works out even with established clients. Like I said, the product may have proved to be a dud before you get to it. There may be other problems that they may just be so busy they can't get your research material to you on time. So if for any reason the project doesn't move ahead, make sure that that first half of your advance is convertible to a kill fee. There are other things you might look for and I'll include stuff on this in the extra bonus, unadvertised bonus that everybody is going to get that attended this call that have to do with limiting the number of drafts. I have a client who is famous for diddling with a copy endlessly. And I know who he is, but we won't mention his name. Right. And we both love him, but God bless his heart. I was looking at some copy I delivered to him last year for a new project. And you know what the final draft number was? 27? 27 drafts of this package. You can't do that and make money. You have to basically say, look, I will give you three drafts. I will give you five drafts. Okay. But there has to be a cutoff. And then finally there's the legal side of it and I'm going, two weeks from now the article in the total package is going to be about the ethics and legality of what we do and how copywriters can stay safe. But it's very important to understand that we are in an increasingly regulated environment, especially in the financial and health areas. And there is liability associated with creating promotions for these areas. And so in every contract I have a clause that says three things. The first one is that the client alone is responsible for the legality of the final copy that's mailed. That means that every statement of fact they will have to have substantiated and backed up and that the testimonials and appropriate disclaimers and all of those things, they are responsible for putting into the copy. I'm not a lawyer. I'm a copywriter. And so I make sure that the client assumes responsibility for what is mailed. Secondly, if somebody buys or uses their product and is harmed in any way or claims to be harmed in any way and sues the company and sues me, okay, two things happen. First, the company holds me harmless, which means they cannot, the contract says, they agree that they cannot come back to me and sue me as part of that action. And then the third thing is that should I be approached or incur any cost because of a regulatory action. Again, there'll be a lot more on this in the total package in two weeks that they indemnify me. And that means that if I have to go to New York like I did one year to the day before 9-11 and go up to the top of the World Trade Center and testify before the Commodities Futures Exchange Commission and hire an attorney and pay travel expenses to do all of that, that the client has to reimburse me for those things. So that gives you about seven or eight key points for contracts. And look for your PDF on this because I think it'll be very helpful. Now, this is probably unnecessary, Julie, because I think we all get the total package. But just in case someone joined us and does not, how do you sign up? Julie's not on. Sorry, I was muted. I don't think anybody wants to hear my dog barking in the background. I wanted to hear it. But we were asking, we're saying everyone gets the total package. But just in case, so they make sure they don't miss this contract article that Clayton is doing, where do we sign up if we're not getting it? www.makepeacetotalpackage.com. Terrific. All right. So we've covered the things to look for in a contract and we're going to get more on that. We're going to get a contract guide and an article on the legal issues in two weeks in the total package. I want to come back to something that we, I think we missed. We were talking about how to make each client more profitable when we talked about raising your fees and selling two packages at the start, the second for 50% of the first, and booking your schedule in advance. The one thing we left out though you had mentioned is outsourcing, which in my first 10 years of copyrighting, this was a totally alien package, an alien concept to me. But what do you outsource? Well, I'm big on compartmentalizing. I have to do it when I'm doing marketing consultation with my clients. And I go in and I find that the president of the company is doing everything, including ordering toilet paper for the company and micromanaging everything. And one of the first things I do is I identify the unique things that that person brings to the job that nobody else can do or that that person does better than everybody else. And then I identify the other things that they do and the level of person in terms of salary that could be hired to do those things or that maybe he already has on staff who could do those other things. And then I prohibit him whenever possible from doing things that he's not the very best at because frankly there are other people who do those things better than he does. And so the same is true in my business as a copywriter. Early on I asked myself the question, what is it that my customers are really hiring me for? What do they want from me? Well they want hot sales copy. And I do that better I think than anyone they could possibly hire. You have to feel that way about yourself. But I feel I do that better than anyone in the world. So they can't do better than to hire me to write copy. But you know what? I'm the world's worst researcher. Now when I'm writing copy I'm making my clients lots and lots and lots of money. And I'm making myself lots of money. But when I'm researching I'm doing a worse job than they could pay somebody a lot less to do and get better research. And so early on I started hiring people like Dave Tomzick and Ray Knight and other people and simply said okay look this month I've got a package on this or next month I have a package on this. Here's $2,000. I want you to give me a full research pack 50, 60 pages that answers all of these questions. And the person would go away and then when I was ready to start my job there I was. I had all of the research I needed. Later on and that was in the days when I wasn't really kind of in the driver's seat with clients so I wasn't able to say look I'll do this but you guys have to give me the research. And so I figured it was cheaper for me to pay $2,000 to somebody else to do this research for me on a package than it was for me to spend 50% of my time writing the package which was $15,000 or $20,000 for that month to do the research myself and I'd end up with a better product. Another thing that I did when I write packages for example for a boardroom book there's a lot of typing involved. They send you a hard copy book that could be four, five, six hundred pages long. You have to read this whole thing and one of the parts of the process is to find the information in the book that is the forehead slapping stuff that says my god I never thought of that, that's brilliant. And then use that as proof elements in your copy. Well that requires a heck of a lot of typing. Again, I don't get paid to type, I get paid to write. I have fortunately these days a person on staff who's a very quick type and does that for me. I simply read through the book, put a post on the pages where I want something taken verbatim out of the book and highlight it and have somebody else do that typing. My assistant does that for me too but we actually use an optical scanner. Yeah, with an optical scanner these days, we tried that. These days it works. These days it actually works, that's right. So the principle here though, no matter what kind of copy you're going to write, is to identify the things that make you the most money and that you do better than anyone else and then make your client the most money and then identify the things that make you a lot less and outsource those things. Either to the client requiring that they provide this material or to people that you pay to do this work for you. So come out my old sayhead, you'll get the work done faster and it'll be better quality work. I always tell people this and especially beginners, but I tell them this people not just copywriters in any field, this philosophy, they look at me like I'm an alien and I say the best way I can explain it, I haven't been to the post office in 12 years, I haven't been to the back in 15 years. Why do that? We can hire some bright high school or a homemaker or a college student or professional for $10 an hour, whatever it costs in your town. I have the best internet researchers in the world for $20 an hour. You can't beat that. So we're on 115 so we're going to have to close, but let me deal with one important issue a couple of people have emailed about and then we'll save the rest of the points and emails for the first half of our next call. The question is this, we've talked about that some copywriters are worried that the client will cheat them out of a royalty and you gave some very good advice about first doing it with clients you can trust, but also asking for the mailing receipts and you explain how that worked. But what if you're still nervous or you're not getting the mailing receipts, what if you still think somehow doesn't seem like I'm getting what I should be getting? Is there anything else you can do at this point? Well there's not a lot. I mean number one, ask for mailing receipts or actual accounting if you're working on commissions. Ask for accounting reports. The minute they issue those they've made a statement as part of the contract and they're liable to it. Secondly, understand and make it clear to them that it's in their best interest to overpay you if anything because if what you do works for them then the only way to keep you on board and keep that money coming in is to keep you happy. Another way to do it is to go ahead and if it's an information product for example, go ahead and subscribe to it. Get on their mailing list and subscribe to other information products and then watch your mailbox. If somebody tells me, and this happened to me with a major big name client that everybody would recognize who I worked for exactly once, I wrote a promotion and they said, well it worked alright. We'll probably mail some. The next month I got one in my mailbox and the next month after that I got two or three and the month after that I got five or six and I'm calling them up and I'm saying, okay guys how many of you mailed? Well we haven't earned out yet. The royalties still don't equal the advance that we paid you. Well that was total baloney and as a result I turned down every future assignment they offered me. They were, and basically they just outsmarted themselves. I lost a little bit of money but they lost a hot copywriter who could have radically grown their business and as a result they ended up really on the ropes. There's still an existence that they're a fraction of the size they were before. Finally if you're suspicious and if you really think that something funny is going on, call around. In fact it might not even be a bad idea to do this before you accept the assignment but talk to other writers. This is another place where networking comes into play and where it's so important. Talk to other writers. See what kind of experiences they've had with this client. If there's been any foul play at all the writer will be more than happy to tell you, whoa they screwed me big time. Absolutely and news gets around. So on that out Julie what's our schedule for our next call? We are next Wednesday, same time, same that case. That would be Wednesday, October 26th at 12 noon Eastern Standard Time. Same call in phone number and same call in passcode. And we thank you for being with us and Clayton, Julie thanks as usual. Thank you guys. Write and let us know how we did. Yes and we will get and if you have more questions send them and they will all be answered. Yes thanks. Thanks everyone. Bye bye. Bye.
